blocks.title: Let's understand how you think and act

questions.text: Your group project is going completely wrong — wrong direction, deadline is tomorrow. What do you do?

options.label: Take charge and redirect the whole team myself
Quietly fix my part and hope others follow
Talk to each person individually to get everyone aligned
Raise the issue openly so everyone pauses and rethinks

questions.text: You've been studying for 3 hours. Your friend calls asking you to come out. You:

options.label: Go — I need the break and time with friends
Say no — I finish what I start
Go for 30 minutes, then come back
Feel guilty either way and can't focus

questions.text: Your teacher marks your answer wrong even though you're certain you're right. You:

options.label: Politely challenge them with your reasoning
Accept it and move on to avoid conflict
Stew about it but say nothing
Double-check yourself first, then decide whether to speak up

questions.text: There's a new student who knows nobody. What do you do?

options.label: Walk up and introduce myself right away
Wait to see if they seem open to talking first
Ask someone else who's more outgoing to include them
Smile and nod — let them settle in on their own

questions.text: You have a week of holidays with no plans. What does your ideal week look like?

options.label: Hanging out with as many friends as possible
Reading, watching documentaries, learning something new
Mostly alone at home — sleep, recharge, peace
Doing something useful — volunteering or helping family

questions.text: You're in the middle of a tough exam and your mind completely blanks. You:

options.label: Panic — heart racing, can't think straight
Skip it, do other questions, come back to it calm
Take a breath, close my eyes, try again methodically
Accept the loss and focus on what's left

questions.text: Your school is planning a big annual event and needs volunteers. You:

options.label: Step up to be the event coordinator
Help with whatever task nobody else wants
Come up with fresh ideas for the theme and entertainment
Help only if a close friend is involved

questions.text: A friend asks you to help them copy your homework. You:

options.label: Refuse and explain why it's a bad idea
Help but feel uncomfortable the whole time
Help without thinking twice — they need it
Offer to explain the concept instead of giving answers

questions.text: Your class gets a surprise test on something you haven't studied. You feel:

options.label: Anxious — this is going to ruin my grade
Annoyed but I'll just wing it
Curious — let's see what I actually know
Calm — one test doesn't define anything

questions.text: You get assigned a big solo project due in 3 weeks. You:

options.label: Make a week-by-week plan immediately
Think about it vaguely and start properly in week 2
Start right away without much planning — I work better under pressure
Get anxious about it for weeks, then rush at the end

questions.text: Two of your friends are in a fight and both want you to take their side. You:

options.label: Try to mediate — hear both out and find common ground
Stay out of it completely — not my problem
Tell both honestly what I think, even if they don't like it
Pick the one I think is right, even if it costs me the friendship

questions.text: You find out you made a mistake that affected your whole team's project marks. You:

options.label: Own it immediately — tell the team and teacher
Feel terrible about it for days
Quietly try to fix it before anyone notices
Move on — mistakes happen, can't change the past

questions.text: You overhear a teacher saying something harsh and unfair about a student. You:

options.label: Speak to the student privately to check they're okay
Report it to the school — it's wrong
It's not my business — I didn't hear it
Feel bothered but unsure what to do

questions.text: You've been given ₹500 as a birthday gift. Most likely you:

options.label: Save it immediately — I have a goal in mind
Spend it on something fun right now
Split it — half save, half spend
Use it to try something I've been curious about

questions.text: You're working on a creative assignment and you get an unusual, risky idea. You:

options.label: Go for it — worst case I fail, but it'll be interesting
Do the safe version first, keep the risky idea as backup
Ask the teacher first before committing
Drop it — I want marks, not experiments

questions.text: Your class is having a debate competition. You:

options.label: Volunteer to speak — debates energise me
Volunteer to do research and prep instead
Participate only if I absolutely have to
Help behind the scenes — manage the flow, time-keep

questions.text: You're asked to give feedback to a friend about their creative work that's genuinely not good. You:

options.label: Be honest even if it's uncomfortable — they need to know
Find something positive to say and skip the negatives
Ask them questions that help them see the issues themselves
Tell them it's good — I hate hurting people

questions.text: You wake up and realise you've overslept and will be 20 minutes late to school. You:

options.label: Panic and rush — this is terrible
Feel bad but move quickly and accept the consequence
Text a friend to cover for me while I figure out a plan
Stay calm — being late once isn't the end of the world

questions.text: When you disagree with a popular opinion in class, you usually:

options.label: Say so clearly — different views need to be heard
Stay quiet — it's not worth the conflict
Raise it as a question rather than a direct challenge
Write it down for myself but don't say it out loud

questions.text: You're working late on something important and a friend keeps messaging asking to hang out. You:

options.label: Keep working — I'll catch up with them later
Stop working and go — the work will wait
Feel torn and can't focus on either properly
Finish the current section, then reply with a time I'm free

questions.text: A classmate takes credit for an idea you came up with. You:

options.label: Correct the record in front of others immediately
Bring it up with them privately after
Let it go — I know the truth, that's enough
Stew about it but say nothing and feel resentful

questions.text: You've been given a choice: work on a topic you know well or explore something completely new for a class project. You:

options.label: Explore the new topic — sounds more interesting
Stick with the familiar — better chance of a good grade
Pick the new topic but research thoroughly first
Ask the teacher which one will be easier to score on

questions.text: After a long day at school you feel most recharged by:

options.label: Calling or meeting friends for a couple of hours
Alone time — room, headphones, no one bothering me
Something physical — a sport, a walk
Something absorbing — a book, a show, a game

questions.text: Your school introduces a new rule you think is unfair. You:

options.label: Organise a petition with classmates to push back
Follow it regardless — rules are rules
Complain to friends but do nothing about it
Raise it respectfully with the teacher or principal

questions.text: You've been asked to present in front of the whole school assembly. You feel:

options.label: Excited — I love having a platform
Nervous but I'll prepare well and get through it
Terrified — I'd rather do anything else
Okay with it — it's just part of school life

blocks.title: What kind of work excites you?

questions.text: If you had to pick one thing to spend a Saturday afternoon on, what would it be?

options.label: Build or fix something with your hands
Research a mystery or unsolved question
Create art, music, or write a story
Organise a get-together or help a friend
Try to sell something or pitch a business idea
Organise your room, plan your week, make lists

questions.text: You're on YouTube. Which video do you click first?

options.label: How a jet engine actually works — engineering explained
Why humans dream — science of the brain
How this 19-year-old made ₹10 lakh from scratch
A spoken word poem that made millions cry
Documentary: people who rebuild lives after disaster
The most efficient way to organise any workspace

questions.text: School gives you a free period — no rules, work on anything. You pick:

options.label: Write a short film script or comic strip
Organise a fundraiser for a cause you care about
Analyse some data or facts you've been curious about
Fix or make something physical — a prop, a model
Map out a detailed plan for something big in your future
Teach a concept you love to a younger student

questions.text: Which school subject do you actually look forward to the most?

options.label: Physics / Chemistry / Biology
Maths
English / Literature / Languages
History / Geography / Social Studies
Business Studies / Economics
Art / Music / Drama

questions.text: You're on a school trip to a new city. You're most excited to:

options.label: Visit a factory or see how industrial things are made
Go to a science museum or planetarium
Wander local markets and chat with vendors about their trade
Explore street art, live music, or local theatre
Visit an NGO or community centre doing real work
Tour the city's civic infrastructure — courts, city hall, hospitals

questions.text: Your dream project is something that:

options.label: Solves a real physical problem — clean water, safer structures
Discovers something nobody has known before
Makes people feel something powerful — a film, a song
Directly improves the lives of real people in need
Builds a company or brand that changes an industry
Creates a perfectly designed system others depend on

questions.text: A friend group is starting a new project together. Which role do you naturally end up in?

options.label: Building or making the actual product
Doing the research — digging up what we need to know
Coming up with the look, feel, and creative direction
Keeping the group motivated and resolving tension
Taking charge of the strategy and making key decisions
Managing the timeline, tasks, and making sure nothing falls through

questions.text: If you had to spend 2 hours helping someone, which would you enjoy most?

options.label: Helping them fix or build something broken
Helping them understand a concept that's confusing them
Helping them express their feelings creatively
Just being there, listening, and giving advice
Helping them figure out a business or money problem
Helping them get organised or manage their time

questions.text: Which of these would you most like to be known for at 30?

options.label: Building something impressive with my own hands
Being an expert who knows more than almost anyone in my field
Creating something beautiful that people still talk about
Being someone who genuinely helped a lot of people
Building a successful business or leading a major team
Running a well-oiled operation that never drops the ball

questions.text: You're most productive when you're:

options.label: Working with tools, equipment, or physical materials
Digging into a complex problem with no clear answer
Making something from scratch with creative freedom
Collaborating closely with people I care about
Leading a team toward a big goal under some pressure
Following a clear process and checking things off a list

questions.text: You get an unexpected free afternoon. Without thinking, you'd probably:

options.label: Tinker with something — fix a bike, try cooking, make something
Go down a rabbit hole reading about something I've been curious about
Sketch, write, play an instrument, or watch a great film
Call someone who might need company or a listening ear
Try to hustle — sell something, plan a project, explore an opportunity
Tidy up, reorganise, or plan the week ahead

questions.text: Which kind of class activity do you enjoy most?

options.label: Lab sessions where we do hands-on experiments
Case studies or problems with no single right answer
Creative assignments — essays, art projects, performances
Group discussions where we hear different perspectives
Competitions or debates where I can persuade people
Structured tasks where I follow clear steps and get results

questions.text: When you imagine a really satisfying workday at age 25, it looks like:

options.label: I built or fixed something that now actually works
I figured out something that was confusing everyone else
I made something that didn't exist before and it's beautiful
Someone told me I genuinely made a difference to them today
I closed a deal or convinced a room full of people to believe in my idea
I ran a complex operation flawlessly without a single error

questions.text: At a family gathering, a relative asks what kind of work you want to do. The most honest answer is:

options.label: Something where I'm building or making real things
Research or analysis — I want to deeply understand something
Something creative — I want to make art or tell stories
Working with people — teaching, counselling, healthcare
Running my own thing or leading a big team
Something stable with clear processes — finance, administration, law

questions.text: If you could shadow someone for a week, whose job would you pick?

options.label: A structural engineer building a bridge
A research scientist running experiments
A film director or graphic novelist
A therapist or social worker
A startup founder or top business negotiator
A chartered accountant or court administrator

questions.text: When a big problem needs solving, you're most likely to:

options.label: Try different physical approaches until something works
Research every angle before doing anything
Think of an unexpected creative angle nobody considered
Get people together and solve it collaboratively
Take charge and push through decisively
Break it into steps and work through them one by one

questions.text: You're most uncomfortable when you have to:

options.label: Sit at a desk for hours without doing anything physical
Make decisions without enough information or data
Follow strict rules that leave zero room for creativity
Work completely alone with no human contact for days
Take orders from someone whose judgment I don't trust
Work without any clear structure, deadlines, or defined goals

questions.text: Which of these sounds most like a fun challenge to you?

options.label: Build a working object using only basic materials
Solve a puzzle that requires pattern recognition and logic
Express a complex emotion through a visual or written piece
Help someone through a hard decision or personal crisis
Convince a skeptical audience to believe in an idea
Audit a process for inefficiencies and fix every single one

questions.text: The best kind of homework for you would be:

options.label: A practical task — build a model, do an experiment
Research an open-ended question and form your own view
Create something original — a poem, short film, illustration
Interview someone in the community about their experience
Present a business or proposal to the class
Complete structured exercises with correct/incorrect answers

questions.text: Which of these news headlines would make you read more?

options.label: Engineers build bridge using only recycled materials
Scientists finally explain why we age — and how to slow it
This 22-year-old's graphic novel won the Booker Prize
Local teacher changes 50 kids' lives through just one lesson
Student turns ₹2000 into a 6-figure business in 18 months
Bank's new fraud detection system catches 99.8% of cases

questions.text: How much can your family invest in college per year (roughly)?

options.label: Under ₹1 lakh
₹1–5 lakh
₹5–15 lakh
₹15 lakh+
We'd consider an education loan for the right college

blocks.title: What do you want your life to look like?

questions.text: Picture yourself at 30. Where are you living?

options.label: A big metro — Mumbai, Delhi, Bangalore
A growing mid-size city
Back in my hometown — I want to build it up
Wherever the work takes me

questions.text: What matters most to you in a career?

options.label: Stability — predictable income, safe job
High income — I want to build serious wealth
Impact — I want to change something real in the world
Flexibility — I want to work on my own terms

questions.text: You have ₹5,000 saved. Option A: Keep it safe in a fixed deposit. Option B: 50% chance you get ₹15,000, 50% chance you lose it all. You:

options.label: Take the fixed deposit — ₹5,000 for sure
Take the gamble — the upside is worth it
Depends on how urgently I need the money right now

questions.text: How do you feel about AI changing jobs in the next 10 years?

options.label: Worried — I want a career that's hard for AI to replace
Excited — I want to work alongside AI and stay ahead of it
Indifferent — I'll adapt to whatever the world becomes

questions.text: Imagine your dream workday at 28. Which sounds closest?

options.label: Meeting clients or speaking to large groups all day
Deep focus work — just me, a problem, and no interruptions
Mixed — some meetings, some independent work
Working in the field — visiting sites, being out and about

questions.text: How important is it that your work feels meaningful and purposeful?

options.label: Essential — I can't do something that feels pointless
Nice but not essential — the money and growth matter more
It depends on the day — both matter at different times
I mostly just want it to be low-stress and sustainable

questions.text: You're 3 years into a stable, well-paying job but you're bored. You:

options.label: Push through — stability matters more than excitement
Start planning an exit toward something more interesting
Look for ways to make the current job more interesting first
Leave and figure it out — life's too short

questions.text: If you could choose, how much would you work per week at 30?

options.label: As much as it takes — I want to build something big
Standard hours — I want a life outside work too
Flexible — some weeks intense, some weeks light
As little as possible — I'd rather earn less and have more time

questions.text: What would genuinely bother you more?

options.label: Earning less than my friends
Working in something I don't believe in
Not being in control of my own time
Feeling like my job could disappear at any moment

questions.text: Your parents want you to take a high-paying but boring career path. You:

options.label: Agree — their experience matters and security is real
Respectfully disagree and make my case with research
Feel torn but probably go with what they say
Do the boring path for a few years, then transition when I'm financially free

questions.text: How do you feel about the idea of starting your own business one day?

options.label: Genuinely exciting — it's something I think about often
Interesting but scary — maybe someday
Not for me — I'd rather be excellent in an existing organisation
Only if I had a genuinely good idea and the right support

questions.text: Which of these worries you most about your future?

options.label: Not making enough money to support my family
Spending my life doing something that doesn't matter
Choosing the wrong career and being stuck
Missing out on experiences because I'm always working
